one_line_takeaway: Augmenting a CLI coding agent with security-focused semantic memory (CodeQL-derived SAST rules for a detector subagent + a CWE-1000 tree for a validator subagent) roughly doubles-plus the rate of correct pre-commit vulnerability review comments (6.9%->17.5%) while cutting false positives 71-85% versus static LLMs and SAST tools, though absolute accuracy stays low and control-flow bugs remain undetected.
key_finding: AgenticSCR achieves the best overall correctness (L&R&T) at 17.5% (RQ1 text also states 17.8%) while emitting far fewer comments (211) than every baseline, giving an absolute +10.6% over the static LLM (6.9%) -- a ~153% relative gain -- and +13.8% over SAST tools, with 71-85% fewer false-positive comments. Ablation (RQ3) shows both security memories matter and are additive: SAST rules in the detector add +5.7% absolute (7.3%->13.0%) and the CWE tree in the validator adds a further +4.5% absolute (13.0%->17.5%), while also cutting comment volume (1,070 -> 362 -> 211). Secure-code-review guidelines help less than SAST rules and, combined with SAST rules, actually slightly hurt (13.0% -> 12.6%). Per type (RQ2), AgenticSCR is best on Injection (23.4%) and Authentication (21.4%), weaker on Information (6.2%) and Resource (16.7%), and Control-flow vulnerabilities are undetected (0%) by every approach.
core_contribution: AgenticSCR is an autonomous agentic-AI framework for PRE-COMMIT secure code review, targeting 'immature vulnerabilities' -- incomplete, latent, context-dependent weaknesses introduced by small incremental code changes before they are committed. Its own contribution (distinct from the cited LLM/SAST prior work summarized in Table 1) is a detector-validator chain of subagents built on Atlassian's RovoDev CLI with claude-sonnet-4@20250514 as the base model: a DETECTOR subagent that localizes/detects/explains vulnerabilities grounded in a security-focused long-term semantic memory of SAST rules (curated from GitHub CodeQL query suites into queryable JSON), and a VALIDATOR subagent that filters false positives using a CWE-1000-derived weakness tree. A second contribution is SCRBench, a repository-aware, human-verified, LINE-LEVEL benchmark of pre-commit immature vulnerabilities. The framework is formalized as a 6-tuple <E,A,G,M,T,Pi> with semantic/working/episodic memory (following Sumers et al.'s cognitive-architecture framing) and CLI tools (open_files, expand_code_chunks, grep, expand_folder, bash/git).
threat_model: Defensive tooling, not an attack paper. The security concern is developer-introduced vulnerabilities at the pre-commit (shift-left) stage, where changes are small and their broader security implications are hard to reason about, so immature vulnerabilities are frequently overlooked and later evolve into exploitable weaknesses (e.g., insecure API usage, missing validation, flawed authorization). Existing pre-commit defenses fail: SAST tools (SonarQube/Semgrep/Snyk) are low-latency but noisy and tuned for mature/complete code; standalone LLMs are limited by context windows and lack repository context and explicit tool use. AgenticSCR is positioned as the earlier-intervention defense that supplies repository context plus authoritative security knowledge (SAST rules + CWE taxonomy) to catch these weaknesses before commit. No adversarial/attacker model against the agent itself is proposed.
limitations: Authors' stated threats: line-level ground truth is subject to annotator bias (mitigated by an operational definition); the relevance metric depends on a probabilistic LLM-as-a-judge (mitigated by 70-comment manual check, F1 0.86); external validity is limited -- SCRBench covers only Python/JS/TS, 5 high-level vulnerability categories, and open-source projects, so results may not generalize to other languages/domains/industrial code, and authors explicitly avoid strong generalization claims. Additional reviewer-observed limits: absolute correctness ceiling is low (17.5% overall; even localization only 30.8%); Control-flow vulnerabilities are entirely missed by all tools (needs business/workflow context absent from code); dataset is small (144 changes / 107 CVEs / 92 repos) and filtered to <=112-line changes; single base model (Claude Sonnet 4) and single agent scaffold (RovoDev), so gains are not isolated from the underlying model; the static-LLM baseline uses Claude 4.5 while the agent uses Sonnet 4, a model mismatch (though this direction is conservative -- the baseline runs on the newer/stronger model yet still loses); and there is a minor internal inconsistency (17.5% in Table 2 vs 17.8% in RQ1 prose, and guideline improvement quoted as both 10.3% and 10.5%/3%).

datasets_used: SCRBench (authors' own): 144 pre-commit code changes spanning 107 CVEs, 92 public GitHub repositories, 33 CWE types, 3 languages (Python/JavaScript/TypeScript); human-verified line-level ground truth
Iannone et al. (2022) CVE collection: 3,256 public CVEs used as the source pool for SCRBench
CWE-1000 taxonomy (used both to build the validator's CWE tree and as the 5 high-level evaluation categories)
GitHub CodeQL query suites (curated into JSON SAST-rule semantic memory)
70 manually labeled code review comments (used to validate the LLM-as-a-judge)
quantitative_results: Overall correctness L&R&T (Table 2): AgenticSCR 17.5% (RQ1 prose 17.8%) with 211 comments; Static LLM 6.9% / 652; CodeQL 2.23.3 0.6% / 1,092; Semgrep 1.140 3.7% / 376; Snyk 1.130 0.9% / 318
Absolute gain over static LLM +10.6% (17.5 vs 6.9) = ~153% relative; over SAST tools +13.8%
False-positive reduction of 71.3%-85% vs static LLM and SAST tools
RQ2 per-type L&R&T for AgenticSCR: Injection 23.4%, Authentication 21.4%, Resource 16.7%, Information 6.2%, Control 0%; Static LLM: 12.6% / 3.3% / 5.0% / 1.5% / 0%
RQ3 ablation L&R&T: Base 7.3% (1,070 cmt) -> Base+SAST 13.0% (362) -> Base+SAST+CWE 17.5% (211); SAST rules +5.7% absolute, CWE tree +4.5% absolute
Guideline variants: Base+Guideline 10.3% (757), Base+Guideline+CWE 12.4% (371), Base+SAST+Guideline 12.6% (604), Base+SAST+Guideline+CWE 14.8% (338)
LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4.1) validation on 70 comments: F1 0.86, Precision 0.88, Recall 0.86
Dataset filters: <=3 pre-commit changes per CVE, <=112 changed lines (median commit size); localization tolerance +/-5 lines
baselines_compared: Static LLM secure reviewer -- Liu et al. [30] SecureReviewer prompt run with Anthropic Claude 4.5 (zero-shot, no fine-tuning)
Internal ablations: Base agentic CLI, +SAST Rules, +CWE Tree, +Guideline combinations (RQ3)
